title: CBP Agents Fatally Shoot U.S. Citizen Alex Pretti During Minneapolis Protest
excerpt: U.S. Border Patrol agents fatally shot 37-year-old Alex Jeffrey Pretti, an ICU nurse and VA employee, in Minneapolis during the ongoing Operation Metro Surge — the second U.S. citizen killed by federal immigration agents in the city in 17 days. Bystander video and eyewitness testimony contradicted federal officials' initial characterization of Pretti as a threat.
content:
On the morning of January 24, 2026, two U.S. Customs and Border Protection officers shot and killed Alex Jeffrey Pretti, a 37-year-old intensive care nurse employed by the Department of Veterans Affairs, near the intersection of 26th Street and Nicollet Avenue in Minneapolis's Whittier neighborhood.

  • Pretti was filming federal agents with his phone and had stepped between an agent and a woman the agent had pushed to the ground when officers pepper-sprayed him, tackled him, and surrounded him with approximately six agents before opening fire.
  • Bystander video verified by Reuters, the BBC, the Wall Street Journal, and other outlets showed Pretti holding only a phone and appearing to shield a woman — not brandishing the licensed firearm he was legally carrying — before agents pinned him down and shot him, firing what eyewitnesses described as approximately 10 shots, including multiple rounds after he was motionless on the ground.
  • DHS Secretary Kristi Noem and White House adviser Stephen Miller immediately labeled Pretti a 'domestic terrorist' and a 'would-be assassin,' characterizations disputed by eyewitnesses, a physician present at the scene, and a CBP internal 'death notification' later reviewed by NPR which offered a substantially different account of the sequence of events.
  • Federal officials blocked state and local investigators from accessing the scene despite a court-ordered search warrant for public areas. A federal judge issued a same-day temporary restraining order barring DHS from destroying or altering evidence.

Pretti's killing — the second fatal shooting of a U.S. citizen by federal immigration agents in Minneapolis in 17 days — sparked nationwide protests, bipartisan congressional calls for a joint investigation, and intensified scrutiny of ICE and CBP use-of-force training and accountability.
